channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Power
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Fourteen agencies attribute a decade-long router campaign to Russia’s FSB Center 16 — energy named as a target sector
item.description: NSA, CISA, the FBI and partners across twelve countries published joint advisory AA26-194A on 13 July, attributing more than a decade of targeting of poorly configured networking devices to FSB Center 16. The tradecraft is deliberately unglamorous: scan internet-facing ranges for SNMP agents that still answer to default or common community strings, issue commands from spoofed source addresses, then copy device configuration files out over TFTP to actor-controlled infrastructure. Cisco Smart Install is a second path. Energy is called out by name. For a utility, the value of a stolen router config is not the router — it is the map: substation addressing, management VLANs, ACLs, tunnel endpoints and the SNMP strings themselves. That is pre-positioning intelligence for a grid intrusion, gathered without touching a relay. - CISA / NSA / FBI joint advisory AA26-194A - Advisory published 13 Jul 2026 — campaign ongoing

item.title: [Critical] SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days exploited in the wild; both added to KEV with a 17 July federal deadline
item.description: Rapid7’s MDR team observed targeted zero-day exploitation of internet-facing SMA 1000 appliances before SonicWall disclosed on 14 July. CVE-2026-15409 (CVSS 10.0) is an unauthenticated server-side request forgery that lets an attacker open a websocket tunnel to localhost-only services; CVE-2026-15410 chains from there, abusing a path-traversal remove_hotfix workflow on port 8188 to execute OS commands as root. CISA added both to the KEV catalog the same day. For transmission and generation operators, SMA-class appliances are frequently the front door for control-room remote access and for OEM support sessions into EMS, relay engineering and DER platforms. An attacker who owns the appliance inherits every session it brokers — including the vendor ones your change process treats as trusted. - CVE-2026-15409 / CVE-2026-15410 - Disclosed & KEV-listed 14 Jul 2026 — exploitation ongoing

item.title: [High] Chinese-made inverters draw a second front: European grid-risk analysis lands as Washington drafts an import ban
item.description: A report published on 11 July argues that with roughly 80% of new European solar installations depending on Chinese-made inverters, a hostile vendor pushing malicious firmware or abusing an undisclosed comms path could act at continental scale. It follows Reuters reporting that rogue communication devices were found inside some Chinese solar and battery inverters deployed in the US, and pv magazine’s 30 June account of a Trump administration ban being drafted; the FCC is separately weighing an import prohibition. WaterISAC also circulated an FBI report on inverter exposure on 9 July. Treat the specific “rogue device” claims as reported rather than independently confirmed — but the structural point stands. DER fleets are managed through vendor clouds and cellular backhaul that most utilities neither own nor monitor. - Industrial Cyber / pv magazine USA / Reuters (via reporting) - European analysis 11 Jul 2026; US ban reported 30 Jun 2026; FBI report circulated 9 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] Hitachi Energy e-mesh EMS carries a critical NGINX heap overflow — the software sitting on top of your DER fleet
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-188-03 (7 July) covers a heap-based buffer overflow reaching e-mesh EMS through a bundled NGINX component, tracked as CVE-2026-42945 with a CVSS v3 base of 8.1 and a CVSS v4 base of 9.2. Successful exploitation could cause an application outage and may permit arbitrary code execution. Versions 4.1.6, 4.4.2 and 4.7.0 are affected. e-mesh EMS is the layer that monitors, dispatches and optimises distributed energy resources — solar, wind, battery storage — so the blast radius is not one asset but the coordination of many. Hitachi Energy’s companion advisory ICSA-26-188-02 for PROMOD V, a transmission planning tool, landed the same day and concerns cleartext handling of sensitive data. - CVE-2026-42945 — CVSS v4 9.2 - Advisory published 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [Critical] July ICS Patch Tuesday: a CVSS 10 in Siemens Opencenter X, critical Logix faults, and a Schneider SCADA code-execution flaw
item.description: Siemens, Schneider Electric and Rockwell Automation shipped their July 2026 Patch Tuesday advisories on 15 July. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical, headlined by a CVSS 10 token-invalidation flaw in Opencenter X that allows full authentication bypass; Mendix, Sidis Secured SmartPlug, Simatic S7-1500, Cadra and Desigo CC also received critical fixes. Schneider addressed a high-severity arbitrary-code-execution flaw in IGSS, its Interactive Graphical SCADA System, plus an authentication bypass in EcoStruxure Cybersecurity Admin Expert. Rockwell issued twelve advisories including three critical DoS conditions that can drive CompactLogix, ControlLogix and GuardLogix controllers into a major non-recoverable fault. VDE CERT added five covering Murrelektronik, Mettler Toledo, Codesys and Wago. - Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider Electric / Rockwell PSIRT / VDE CERT / SecurityWeek - Advisories published 15 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Siemens SICAM 8 fix closes a firmware-update path that could plant a persistent backdoor on substation RTUs
item.description: Siemens published an advisory on 9 July covering four SICAM 8 vulnerabilities; CISA republished it as ICSA-26-197-05 on 16 July. The one that matters most is CVE-2026-54799 (CVSS v3 7.2), which turns the trusted firmware-update process itself into an attack vector — an attacker in a position to abuse it could install malicious firmware and hold persistent control of the device. The affected families are SICAM A8000, SICAM EGS and SICAM S8000, widely deployed as RTUs and gateways in substations and along distribution feeders. Fixes ship in CPCI85 firmware V26.20 and SICORE V26.20.0. A CVSS of 7.2 undersells the operational reality here: firmware persistence on a substation RTU survives the reboot, the re-image and, usually, the investigation. - CVE-2026-54799 — CVSS v3 7.2 - Siemens advisory 9 Jul 2026; CISA republished 16 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] CISA urges SharePoint hardening after fresh exploitation — four flaws added to KEV on 14 July
item.description: CISA published a SharePoint hardening alert on 14 July following new exploitation, and added four actively exploited vulnerabilities to the KEV catalog the same day: SonicWall’s CVE-2026-15409 and CVE-2026-15410 alongside Microsoft’s CVE-2026-56155 (AD FS privilege escalation) and CVE-2026-56164 (SharePoint missing authentication for a critical function, exploitable over the network). Federal agencies face remediation deadlines of 17 and 28 July. This is an IT story with an OT tail. On-premises SharePoint farms in utilities routinely hold single-line diagrams, relay setting files, NERC CIP evidence, switching orders and vendor contact lists — and AD FS is often the identity broker in front of the engineering applications that matter. - CVE-2026-56164 / CVE-2026-56155 - Alert & KEV additions 14 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] Schneider Easergy MiCOM Px40 relays: hard-coded credentials expose device data over SNMP
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-190-03 (9 July) covers CVE-2026-4832, a use-of-hard-coded-credentials issue rated CVSS v3 5.3 in the Easergy MiCOM Px40 protection relay series used across medium, high and extra-high voltage protection. An unauthenticated attacker able to interrogate the SNMP port can retrieve sensitive device information. Affected firmware: P14x prior to B4A, P24x prior to D3A, P341 prior to E3F, and P342/P343/P344/P345 prior to B3F. On its own this is an information-disclosure finding with a modest score. Read it next to this week’s FSB Center 16 advisory and it is something else: an SNMP-reachable protection relay handing over device detail to precisely the scanning tradecraft fourteen agencies just described. - CVE-2026-4832 — CVSS v3 5.3 - Advisory published 9 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] Acting CISA chief: plan for adversarial disruption of critical infrastructure rather than assume you can prevent it
item.description: Speaking at the ICS Village and Institute for Security and Technology Critical Effect conference, acting CISA director Nick Andersen said the US should expect “an adversarial disruption of our critical infrastructure” with significant technical and psychological impact, and that operators “are not going to be able to keep everything persistently online and available as much as we would like.” He argued assumptions should change accordingly — including planning on the basis that telecommunications infrastructure will be disrupted. For grid operators the message is not new, but the source is notable: the federal posture has moved decisively from prevention toward resilience. Andersen also expressed scepticism that new mandates are the answer, pointing instead to state and local grant funding and prioritised outreach. - Cybersecurity Dive (Critical Effect conference) - Reported 17 Jun 2026
